jarvis.name: jarvis
jarvis.title: Jarvis
jarvis.subtitle: ai server
jarvis.description: Personal AI orchestrator running on a Mac Studio. Discord bot + judge loop + overnight Claude Code runs. Notes on the build.

posts.title: Giving Jarvis a screen — Ghost OS
posts.summary: Wiring in macOS GUI automation as an MCP tool — visual verification, a wiki auditor, recipes — while keeping it strictly optional and serialized on one screen.

posts.title: Reaching outside the box — MCP and ingest
posts.summary: Delegating the brain's ingest workflow to a Claude Code session, wiring user-scoped MCP tools, and the two routing regressions that followed.

posts.title: Running it overnight
posts.summary: Queue, resource gates, memory monitoring, crash recovery, notification coalescing — and the three bugs the acceptance test surfaced first.

posts.title: The worker framework
posts.summary: A supervisor with exponential backoff, two watchdog-based watchers, four clock-driven workers, and the Whisper config that lied about Apple Silicon.

posts.title: The Discord bot
posts.summary: Channel-aware routing, the five paths a message can take, the privacy gate, and the source footer that tells you where every answer came from.

posts.title: RAG over a personal vault
posts.summary: BGE embeddings, ChromaDB, paragraph-boundary chunking, and a privacy-tag filter that keeps personal notes off the network.

posts.title: The judge loop
posts.summary: A local Qwen 2.5 32B reads Claude Code's diffs and decides whether to accept them. The bugs that broke it twice, plus the loop that wouldn't converge.

posts.title: Building the orchestrator
posts.summary: Worktrees, the test runner, the iteration loop — and the duplicate-run-row bug that lived at the seam between the CLI and the queue.

posts.title: What Jarvis actually is
posts.summary: The architecture in one paragraph, then the parts that actually mattered. A local router, five paths, six background workers.

posts.title: Why the Mac Studio
posts.summary: I almost returned the M3 Ultra. Here's what changed my mind about keeping a 96GB unified memory machine.

atlas.name: atlas
atlas.title: Atlas
atlas.subtitle: self-hosted chatgpt
atlas.description: A web app that puts my local models, my vault, and tool calls behind a single chat interface. Same machine as Jarvis, different shape.

posts.title: Watching the judge loop fail
posts.summary: Atlas was built by the orchestrator's judge loop. Every way it failed — context overflow, hallucinated evidence, suppressed test names, no memory — was about inputs, not reasoning.

posts.title: Tunneling and auth for a one-person app
posts.summary: Cloudflare Tunnel + Supabase magic links. Why this is overkill for one user and why I did it anyway.

posts.title: Tools and images, without the leaks
posts.summary: Weather, places, and web search with mock fallbacks when keys are absent; image upload that keeps the bytes on the machine by construction.

posts.title: One large model at a time
posts.summary: 96GB sounds like a lot until you load two 70B models. The model manager — and the long stretch where it didn't actually manage anything.

posts.title: The router and the Gemini path
posts.summary: A pattern-matching router, a quota-tracked Gemini client, and the missing load_dotenv() that made the Gemini path silently not exist.

posts.title: Hello, Atlas
posts.summary: Why a self-hosted ChatGPT made sense on top of an existing AI server, and why it's a separate codebase from Jarvis.
